abstract: Deep learning optimizers are often motivated through a mix of convex and approximate second-order theory. We select three such methods -- Adam, Shampoo and Prodigy -- and argue that each method can instead be understood as a squarely first-order method without convexity assumptions. In fact, after switching off exponential moving averages, each method is equivalent to steepest descent under a particular norm. By generalizing this observation, we chart a new design space for training algorithms. Different operator norms should be assigned to different tensors based on the role that the tensor plays within the network. For example, while linear and embedding layers may have the same weight space of $\mathbb{R}^{m\times n}$, these layers play different roles and should be assigned different norms. We hope that this idea of carefully metrizing the neural architecture might lead to more stable, scalable and indeed faster training.

keyInsights: Popular deep learning optimizers implicitly perform steepest descent in carefully defined metric spaces by choosing specific norms for their updates.
Optimizer design can be fundamentally decoupled into two distinct problems: selecting an appropriate norm to define the geometry of the weight space, and determining an effective step size.
Generalizing this, intentionally assigning different operator norms to distinct layers (tensors) within a neural network can create a principled design space for new algorithms.
originalProblem: Existing theoretical frameworks for deep learning optimizers are often complex, relying on heuristics, convex optimization, or approximate second-order methods.
The underlying geometric choices defining how these optimizers update weights are often implicit and not fully understood.
A lack of principled guidance for designing new optimizers or systematically improving existing ones beyond empirical tuning.
results: Adam, when EMAs are disabled, is reinterpreted as steepest descent under the max-of-max norm, explaining its implicit per-matrix gradient normalization.
Shampoo, without its accumulation mechanism, is shown to perform steepest descent under the max spectral norm over layers, supported by a majorization-minimization framework.
Prodigy, without EMAs, also reduces to sign gradient descent, with its adaptive step size reinterpreted as a heuristic for automatic "escape velocity" determination.
solution: Disabling Exponential Moving Averages (EMAs) in Adam, Shampoo, and Prodigy to isolate their core update mechanisms.
Mathematically demonstrating that the simplified update rules of these optimizers are equivalent to exact first-order steepest descent under specific, implicitly chosen norms.
Proposing a "modular norm" framework that allows for intentional assignment of different operator norms to individual layers or tensors within a neural network.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at MIT CSAIL reinterpret prominent deep learning optimizers like Adam, Shampoo, and Prodigy, demonstrating that, by disabling Exponential Moving Averages, each performs exact first-order steepest descent under specific, implicitly chosen norms. This unified framework simplifies their theoretical understanding and proposes a systematic design space for new optimization algorithms based on intentional norm assignment.

title: Old Optimizer, New Norm: An Anthology

abstract:
Optimization with matrix gradient orthogonalization has recently demonstrated
impressive results in the training of deep neural networks (Jordan et al.,
2024; Liu et al., 2025). In this paper, we provide a theoretical analysis of
this approach. In particular, we show that the orthogonalized gradient method
can be seen as a first-order trust-region optimization method, where the
trust-region is defined in terms of the matrix spectral norm. Motivated by this
observation, we develop the stochastic non-Euclidean trust-region gradient
method with momentum, which recovers the Muon optimizer (Jordan et al., 2024)
as a special case, along with normalized SGD and signSGD with momentum
(Cutkosky and Mehta, 2020; Sun et al., 2023). In addition, we prove
state-of-the-art convergence results for the proposed algorithm in a range of
scenarios, which involve arbitrary non-Euclidean norms, constrained and
composite problems, and non-convex, star-convex, first- and second-order smooth
functions. Finally, our theoretical findings provide an explanation for several
practical observations, including the practical superiority of Muon compared to
the Orthogonal-SGDM algorithm of Tuddenham et al. (2022) and the importance of
weight decay in the training of large-scale language models.

keyInsights: Gradient orthogonalization (e.g., orth(G)) is precisely equivalent to a first-order trust-region gradient method where the trust region is defined using the matrix spectral norm, correcting previous inaccurate theoretical explanations.

The order of operations (momentum *before* orthogonalization, as in Muon) is critical for effective variance reduction and practical superiority, explaining why Muon outperforms methods like Orthogonal-SGDM.

Weight decay is theoretically shown to help bound iterates and improve convergence rates (1/ε^3 for star-convex functions), supporting its observed crucial role in training large language models with Muon.
originalProblem: Recent gradient orthogonalization optimizers, like Muon, achieved strong empirical success in deep learning, especially for LLMs, but lacked a rigorous and accurate theoretical understanding.
Existing theoretical interpretations for gradient orthogonalization methods were noted as insufficient or incorrect, leaving the underlying mechanisms unexplained.

A unified theoretical framework was needed to connect gradient orthogonalization methods with other established optimizers like normalized SGD and signSGD, and to explain practical observations such as the role of weight decay.
results: The proposed generalized algorithm achieves a 1/ε^4 iteration complexity for finding an ε-stationary point in non-convex settings, representing the first convergence result for Muon and matching existing optimal SGD-type complexities.

For star-convex functions, a variant with weight decay achieves an improved 1/ε^3 iteration complexity for reaching an ε-suboptimal solution, outperforming prior results for related non-convex second-order smooth functions.

A variant incorporating extrapolation achieves a 1/ε^3.5 complexity for second-order smooth non-convex functions, matching previous results for normalized SGD and signSGD under these stronger assumptions.
solution: The paper re-conceptualizes gradient orthogonalization as a first-order non-Euclidean trust-region optimization method, specifically using the matrix spectral norm for the trust region.

A generalized algorithm, the 'Stochastic Non-Euclidean Trust-Region Gradient Method with Momentum,' is proposed, which unifies Muon (with spectral norm), normalized SGD (with Euclidean norm), and signSGD (with infinity norm).

Variants of the algorithm are introduced with weight decay for star-convex functions and with extrapolation for second-order smooth functions, allowing for analysis across diverse optimization scenarios.
paper_summary.summary: This research from Yandex Research provides a rigorous theoretical interpretation of gradient orthogonalization methods, such as Muon, by demonstrating their equivalence to a first-order trust-region optimization method defined with respect to non-Euclidean norms. The work unifies several gradient-based optimizers, establishing state-of-the-art convergence guarantees for non-convex, star-convex, and second-order smooth objective functions, and explains practical phenomena like Muon's empirical success and the importance of weight decay in large language model training.

title: Understanding Gradient Orthogonalization for Deep Learning via  Non-Euclidean Trust-Region Optimization

abstract: In this paper, we investigate the properties of the Sliced Wasserstein Distance (SW) when employed as an objective functional. The SW metric has gained significant interest in the optimal transport and machine learning literature, due to its ability to capture intricate geometric properties of probability distributions while remaining computationally tractable, making it a valuable tool for various applications, including generative modeling and domain adaptation. Our study aims to provide a rigorous analysis of the critical points arising from the optimization of the SW objective. By computing explicit perturbations, we establish that stable critical points of SW cannot concentrate on segments. This stability analysis is crucial for understanding the behaviour of optimization algorithms for models trained using the SW objective. Furthermore, we investigate the properties of the SW objective, shedding light on the existence and convergence behavior of critical points. We illustrate our theoretical results through numerical experiments.
title: Towards Understanding Gradient Dynamics of the Sliced-Wasserstein Distance via Critical Point Analysis

abstract: Spectral gradient methods, such as the recently popularized Muon optimizer, are a promising alternative to standard Euclidean gradient descent for training deep neural networks and transformers, but it is still unclear in which regimes they are expected to perform better. We propose a simple layerwise condition that predicts when a spectral update yields a larger decrease in the loss than a Euclidean gradient step. This condition compares, for each parameter block, the squared nuclear-to-Frobenius ratio of the gradient to the stable rank of the incoming activations. To understand when this condition may be satisfied, we first prove that post-activation matrices have low stable rank at Gaussian initialization in random feature regression, feedforward networks, and transformer blocks. In spiked random feature models we then show that, after a short burn-in, the Euclidean gradient's nuclear-to-Frobenius ratio grows with the data dimension while the stable rank of the activations remains bounded, so the predicted advantage of spectral updates scales with dimension. We validate these predictions in synthetic regression experiments and in NanoGPT-scale language model training, where we find that intermediate activations have low-stable-rank throughout training and the corresponding gradients maintain large nuclear-to-Frobenius ratios. Together, these results identify conditions for spectral gradient methods, such as Muon, to be effective in training deep networks and transformers.

keyInsights: Spectral updates offer greater loss reduction than Euclidean steps when the gradient's nuclear-to-Frobenius ratio is greater than or equal to the activation's stable rank.
Mean-spike inducing nonlinearities (e.g., ReLU) and transformer token embeddings naturally generate and propagate low stable rank in internal activation matrices, often bounded by small constants.

The nuclear rank of gradients, initially small, rapidly increases to scale linearly with ambient dimension after a few training steps and persists at this high level for substantial training durations.
originalProblem: Lack of a clear theoretical understanding explaining the empirical successes of spectral optimizers, such as Muon, in deep learning.
Identifying specific regimes and conditions under which spectral gradient updates offer an advantage over conventional Euclidean gradient methods.
Bridging the gap between the observed performance of spectral methods and the underlying mathematical principles governing deep learning optimization dynamics.
results: A quantifiable layerwise condition, `nr(G_ℓ) ≥ st(A_{ℓ-1})`, consistently predicts when spectral updates are beneficial, verified both theoretically and empirically.

Empirical analysis on NanoGPT-scale transformers confirms that internal MLP and RMS-normalized activations maintain low stable ranks (typically single-digit to low-tens), while corresponding gradients exhibit high nuclear ranks (often in the hundreds) throughout training.

Spectral methods are specifically justified for internal MLP and attention blocks in transformers due to these combined structural properties, explaining their observed performance benefits in these contexts.
solution: Established a layerwise condition for spectral advantage by comparing Euclidean and spectral gradient descent steps based on the gradient's nuclear rank and the activation's stable rank.
Developed a "calculus" to analyze and propagate the stable rank of post-activation matrices through various architectural components like nonlinearities, normalization, and residual connections.
Investigated the dynamic evolution of gradient nuclear rank during training using random feature models and validated findings on synthetic and transformer models.
paper_summary.summary: A theoretical framework identifies conditions for spectral gradient methods to surpass Euclidean approaches in deep learning optimization. The work demonstrates that spectral updates are advantageous when the gradient's nuclear rank is sufficiently high relative to the low stable rank of activation matrices, a condition frequently met in modern neural networks like transformers.

title: When do spectral gradient updates help in deep learning?

abstract: In this paper, we introduce a model for analyzing deep learning optimization over a single iteration by leveraging the matrix structure of the weights. We derive the model by assuming isotropy of curvature, including the second-order Hessian and higher-order terms, of the loss function across all perturbation directions; hence, we call it the isotropic curvature model. This model is a convex optimization program amenable to analysis, which allows us to understand how an update on the weights in the form of a matrix relates to the change in the total loss function. As an application, we use the isotropic curvature model to analyze the recently introduced Muon optimizer and other matrix-gradient methods for training language models. First, we show that under a general growth condition on the curvature, the optimal update matrix is obtained by making the spectrum of the original gradient matrix more homogeneous -- that is, making its singular values closer in ratio -- which in particular improves the conditioning of the update matrix. Next, we show that the orthogonalized gradient becomes optimal for the isotropic curvature model when the curvature exhibits a phase transition in growth. Taken together, these results suggest that the gradient orthogonalization employed in Muon and other related methods is directionally correct but may not be strictly optimal. Finally, we discuss future research on how to leverage the isotropic curvature model for designing new optimization methods for training deep learning and language models.

keyInsights: The optimal update matrix for a weight layer must align its singular spaces (left and right singular vectors) with those of the original gradient matrix.

Optimal updates generally lead to 'spectrum homogenization,' where the singular values of the update matrix are more equalized in ratio compared to the gradient's singular values, while preserving their relative ordering.
Full gradient orthogonalization, as implemented in Muon, is strictly optimal only under the asymptotic and extreme condition of a 'kink' or sharp phase transition in the curvature function's growth.
originalProblem: A lack of comprehensive theoretical understanding for the empirical success and operational mechanisms of new matrix-gradient optimizers like Muon.
The 'surprising' effectiveness of gradient orthogonalization, which discards magnitude information (singular values), contradicting conventional optimization wisdom.
The need for a principled theoretical framework to guide the design of more effective optimizers beyond empirical trial-and-error.
results: Proposition 3.1 shows that the optimal update matrix `Q*` shares the same singular spaces as the gradient `G`, justifying a key design aspect of Muon.
Theorem 1 establishes that under a super-quadratic curvature growth assumption, optimal updates result in 'spectrum homogenization,' reducing the disparity between singular values.
Theorem 2 and Proposition 3.4 demonstrate that full gradient orthogonalization is optimal if and only if the curvature function `H(r)` exhibits a sudden, sharp change in its derivative (a 'kink').
solution: Introduced the isotropic curvature model, a novel convex optimization program designed to model a single step of deep learning optimization for weight matrices.
Derived the model by applying a Taylor expansion to per-sample losses and making key isotropy assumptions about curvature and input data vectors.
Analyzed the derived model using convex analysis and mathematical tools to characterize the singular spaces and spectrum of the optimal update matrix.
paper_summary.summary: Weijie Su of the University of Pennsylvania developed the isotropic curvature model, a theoretical framework to explain the effectiveness of matrix-gradient optimizers like Muon. This model rigorously demonstrates that optimal update strategies involve aligning singular spaces with the gradient and homogenizing its spectrum, providing a theoretical justification for Muon's design and suggesting a nuanced understanding of when full orthogonalization is optimal.

title: Isotropic Curvature Model for Understanding Deep Learning Optimization: Is Gradient Orthogonalization Optimal?

abstract: Norm-constrained linear minimization oracle (LMO)-based optimizers such as spectral gradient descent and Muon are attractive in large-scale learning, but extending them to manifold-constrained problems is nontrivial and often leads to nested-loop schemes that solve tangent-space subproblems iteratively. We propose \emph{Manifold Constrained Steepest Descent} (MCSD), a single-loop framework for optimization over manifolds that selects a norm-induced steepest-descent direction via an LMO applied to the Riemannian gradient, and then returns to the manifold via projection. Under standard smoothness assumptions, we establish convergence guarantees for MCSD and a stochastic momentum variant. We further introduce \emph{SPEL}, the spectral-norm specialization of MCSD on the Stiefel manifold, which admits scalable implementations via fast matrix sign computations. Experiments on PCA, orthogonality-constrained CNNs, and manifold-constrained LLM adapter tuning demonstrate improved stability and competitive performance relative to standard Riemannian baselines and existing manifold-aware LMO methods.

keyInsights: Decoupling the LMO direction computation in ambient space from the manifold projection enables a computationally efficient single-loop optimization scheme.
The framework provides a method to integrate the benefits of LMO-based Euclidean optimizers with the geometric constraints of manifold optimization while maintaining theoretical soundness.
For the Stiefel manifold, using the spectral norm LMO in conjunction with the matrix sign map for projection results in a highly efficient and effective algorithm.
originalProblem: Efficiently optimizing objective functions over smooth manifolds, which are common in machine learning, poses a significant challenge.
Existing approaches that extend Linear Minimization Oracle (LMO)-based methods to manifolds often require computationally expensive nested-loop schemes for tangent-space subproblems.
Many LMO-based manifold optimization methods also lack formal theoretical convergence guarantees.
results: Theoretical analysis confirms sublinear convergence rates for both deterministic ($O(1/\sqrt{T})$) and stochastic ($O(T^{-1/4})$) MCSD variants for non-convex objectives.
SPEL achieved comparable accuracy to existing Manifold Muon variants and outperformed Riemannian Gradient Descent in PCA, while being substantially faster in wall-clock time.
SPEL obtained the best final test accuracy (79.75%) on CIFAR-100 for orthogonality-constrained Wide ResNet-28 and matched performance in LLM adapter tuning with a lighter optimizer state.
solution: Introduces Manifold Constrained Steepest Descent (MCSD), a single-loop framework that computes an LMO direction from the Riemannian gradient in the ambient Euclidean space.
Updates are performed by taking a step in the LMO direction and then projecting the result back onto the manifold using an efficient nearest-point projection.
SPEL, a specialization for the Stiefel manifold using the spectral norm, leverages the efficient matrix sign map for projection, ensuring Stiefel feasibility with low computational overhead.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at the University of Hong Kong developed Manifold Constrained Steepest Descent (MCSD), a single-loop optimization framework for smooth manifolds, and its spectral-norm variant SPEL for the Stiefel manifold. This method demonstrates superior computational efficiency and achieves competitive or higher accuracy in tasks like orthogonal CNN training and LLM adapter tuning compared to prior manifold optimization techniques.

title: Manifold constrained steepest descent

abstract: A central question in modern deep learning is how to design optimizers whose behavior remains stable as the network width $w$ increases. We address this question by interpreting several widely used neural-network optimizers, including \textrm{AdamW} and \textrm{Muon}, as instances of steepest descent under matrix operator norms. This perspective links optimizer geometry with the Lipschitz structure of the network forward map, and enables width-independent control of both Lipschitz and smoothness constants. However, steepest-descent rules induced by standard $p \to q$ operator norms lack layerwise composability and therefore cannot provide width-independent bounds in deep architectures. We overcome this limitation by introducing a family of mean-normalized operator norms, denoted $\pmean \to \qmean$, that admit layerwise composability, yield width-independent smoothness bounds, and give rise to practical optimizers such as \emph{rescaled} \textrm{AdamW}, row normalization, and column normalization. The resulting learning rate width-aware scaling rules recover $\mu$P scaling~\cite{yang2021tensor} as a special case and provide a principled mechanism for cross-width learning-rate transfer across a broad class of optimizers. We further show that \textrm{Muon} can suffer an $\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{w})$ worst-case growth in the smoothness constant, whereas a new family of row-normalized optimizers we propose achieves width-independent smoothness guarantees. Based on the observations, we propose MOGA (Matrix Operator Geometry Aware), a width-aware optimizer based only on row/column-wise normalization that enables stable learning-rate transfer across model widths. Large-scale pre-training on GPT-2 and LLaMA shows that MOGA, especially with row normalization, is competitive with Muon while being notably faster in large-token and low-loss regimes.

keyInsights: Traditional $p \to q$ operator norms generally fail to provide width-independent Lipschitz control across multiple neural network layers due to dimension-dependent geometric mismatches.

Mean-normalized operator norms achieve width-independent Lipschitz bounds, and width-independent smoothness is secured when the output norm parameter $q$ is at least twice the input norm parameter $p$ ($q \ge 2p$), revealing Muon's $(2,\text{mean}) \to (2,\text{mean})$ geometry can lead to an $O(\sqrt{w})$ smoothness constant growth.

MOGA's principled scaling, rooted in optimization geometry (Lipschitz/smoothness control), not only recovers Maximal Update Parametrization (\muP) scaling for Adam/SignSGD but also extends to scenarios where \muP's spectral assumptions might not hold.
originalProblem: Optimal learning rates for widely used optimizers like AdamW and Muon exhibit strong width-dependency, impeding reliable hyperparameter transfer across different neural network scales.
The necessity for extensive and computationally expensive re-tuning of optimization hyperparameters hinders the practical application of neural scaling laws.
A unified theoretical framework is lacking to interpret various first-order optimizers and predict their scaling behavior with network width based on fundamental geometric properties.
results: MOGA with row normalization demonstrates reliable learning-rate transferability across GPT-2 models (Small to XL), achieving optimal performance with nearly identical peak learning rates, confirming width invariance.

In large-scale language model pre-training, MOGA achieves performance comparable to or exceeding Muon and AdamW, showing particular advantages in the low-loss regime and under large token budgets on LLaMA-130M.

The analysis identifies $(1,\text{mean}) \to (q,\text{mean})$ with $q \ge 2$ (column normalization) and $(p,\text{mean}) \to \infty$ (row normalization) as optimal geometries for achieving width-independent smoothness bounds, guiding the design of more stable optimizers.
solution: A unified geometric framework is established, interpreting optimizers as steepest descent under different matrix operator norms, including $\ell_1 \to \ell_\infty$ for AdamW and $\ell_2 \to \ell_2$ for Muon.

Mean-normalized operator norms are introduced to ensure width-independent Lipschitz continuity and, under specific conditions ($q \ge 2p$), width-independent smoothness constants for the loss function across multiple network layers.

A novel optimizer, MOGA (Matrix Operator Geometry Aware), is proposed, utilizing specific row/column-wise normalizations derived from these mean-normalized norms to facilitate stable learning-rate transfer.
paper_summary.summary: This research introduces a geometric framework based on matrix operator norms to analyze neural network optimizers, deriving a new optimizer, MOGA, that enables stable learning-rate transfer and improved efficiency for large-scale language models by ensuring width-independent Lipschitz and smoothness control of the loss landscape. Experiments demonstrate MOGA's ability to maintain optimal performance across varied model widths with a single learning rate.

title: On the Width Scaling of Neural Optimizers Under Matrix Operator Norms I: Row/Column Normalization and Hyperparameter Transfer

abstract: The pursuit of faster optimization algorithms remains an active and important research direction in deep learning. Recently, the Muon optimizer [JJB+24] has demonstrated promising empirical performance, but its theoretical foundation remains less understood. In this paper, we bridge this gap and provide a theoretical analysis of Muon by placing it within the Lion-$\mathcal{K}$ family of optimizers [CLLL24]. Specifically, we show that Muon corresponds to Lion-$\mathcal{K}$ when equipped with the nuclear norm, and we leverage the theoretical results of Lion-$\mathcal{K}$ to establish that Muon (with decoupled weight decay) implicitly solves an optimization problem that enforces a constraint on the spectral norm of weight matrices. This perspective not only demystifies the implicit regularization effects of Muon but also leads to natural generalizations through varying the choice of convex map $\mathcal{K}$, allowing for the exploration of a broader class of implicitly regularized and constrained optimization algorithms.

keyInsights: The Muon optimizer's matrix sign function update corresponds precisely to a subgradient of the nuclear norm, thereby mapping Muon to a specific instance within the Lion-K framework.
Decoupled weight decay, when used with Muon, implicitly forces the optimizer to solve a problem under a spectral norm constraint on its weight matrices, bounding their largest singular value.

The Lion-K framework offers a principled methodology for generalizing existing optimizers like Muon and designing new ones by varying the choice of the convex function K, each inducing different implicit regularization effects.
originalProblem: New optimizers like Muon demonstrate strong empirical performance but lack clear theoretical underpinnings and a precise understanding of their success mechanisms.
A theoretical gap exists in explaining Muon's behavior, especially its implicit regularization effects, despite its promising results in large-scale model training.

The broader field of optimization algorithms needed a unified theoretical framework to connect modern, empirically successful optimizers to established optimization theories and provide formal guarantees.
results: The Muon optimizer implicitly enforces a spectral norm constraint (||X||_op <= 1/λ) on weight matrices, which is empirically validated to be rapidly and consistently maintained across various deep learning models including ResNet, ViT, and LLaMA.

Convergence rates for the KKT score function were established as O(1/√T) for deterministic gradients and O(1/√T + σ/√nbatch) for stochastic gradients, with almost sure convergence of Muon iterates to the set of KKT points of the implicitly constrained optimization problem.

Comparative studies on LLaMA 0.5B demonstrate that Muon consistently yields more regularized (compact and bounded) singular value distributions for key weight matrices compared to AdamW, which exhibits no such implicit regularization.
solution: The Muon optimizer is reinterpreted and formally integrated into the Lion-K theoretical framework by identifying its update rule with a specific instance using the nuclear norm as the convex function K.
A theoretical connection is established between Muon's use of decoupled weight decay and the implicit enforcement of a spectral norm constraint on weight matrices during optimization.

Rigorous convergence guarantees are derived for Muon's iterates, demonstrating almost sure convergence to Karush–Kuhn–Tucker points of the implicitly constrained optimization problem using Lyapunov functions.
paper_summary.summary: The Muon optimizer implicitly enforces a spectral norm constraint on weight matrices during training, achieved through its integration into the Lion-K theoretical framework and the use of decoupled weight decay. This work provides convergence guarantees for Muon, demonstrating that its iterates converge to Karush–Kuhn–Tucker points of an implicitly constrained optimization problem, and empirically validates these regularization effects across diverse deep learning models.

title: Muon Optimizes Under Spectral Norm Constraints

abstract: Normalization layers (e.g., Batch Normalization, Layer Normalization) were introduced to help with optimization difficulties in very deep nets, but they clearly also help generalization, even in not-so-deep nets. Motivated by the long-held belief that flatter minima lead to better generalization, this paper gives mathematical analysis and supporting experiments suggesting that normalization (together with accompanying weight-decay) encourages GD to reduce the sharpness of loss surface. Here "sharpness" is carefully defined given that the loss is scale-invariant, a known consequence of normalization. Specifically, for a fairly broad class of neural nets with normalization, our theory explains how GD with a finite learning rate enters the so-called Edge of Stability (EoS) regime, and characterizes the trajectory of GD in this regime via a continuous sharpness-reduction flow.
title: Understanding the Generalization Benefit of Normalization Layers: Sharpness Reduction

abstract: Gradient dynamics play a central role in determining the stability and generalization of deep neural networks. In this work, we provide an empirical analysis of how variance and standard deviation of gradients evolve during training, showing consistent changes across layers and at the global scale in convolutional networks. Motivated by these observations, we propose a hyperparameter-free gradient normalization method that aligns gradient scaling with their natural evolution. This approach prevents unintended amplification, stabilizes optimization, and preserves convergence guarantees. Experiments on the challenging CIFAR-100 benchmark with ResNet-20, ResNet-56, and VGG-16-BN demonstrate that our method maintains or improves test accuracy even under strong generalization. Beyond practical performance, our study highlights the importance of directly tracking gradient dynamics, aiming to bridge the gap between theoretical expectations and empirical behaviors, and to provide insights for future optimization research.

keyInsights: Layer-wise gradient standard deviations exhibit highly variable and often irregular behaviors across different layers and network architectures during training, making purely local normalization strategies problematic.

The global standard deviation of gradients, aggregated across all eligible layers, consistently decreases as training progresses, providing a stable, network-wide dynamic for a robust scaling approach.

A hyperparameter-free global scaling factor that diminishes over time, combined with layer-wise zero-centering, can stabilize optimization and improve generalization without unintended gradient amplification.
originalProblem: Deep neural network training often suffers from instability due to vanishing and exploding gradients, affecting convergence and generalization.
A lack of comprehensive empirical understanding of how gradient standard deviation evolves across layers and globally during the entire training process.
Existing layer-wise gradient normalization methods can lead to instability by inadvertently amplifying gradients in layers with very small variances.
results: Gradient Autoscaled Normalization (GAN) consistently improved Top-1 test accuracy on ResNet-20 (from 0.5932 to 0.6134) and ResNet-56 (from 0.7001 to 0.7129) on the CIFAR-100 benchmark.

The method maintained comparable performance on VGG-16-BN (0.7454 vs. baseline 0.7454), demonstrating robustness and avoiding degradation even in challenging experimental setups with strong regularization.

GAN outperformed other gradient normalization techniques, including GradNorm, Gradient Centralization, and Z-Score Normalization, which often yielded degraded or inconsistent results compared to the AdamW baseline.
solution: The paper introduces Gradient Autoscaled Normalization (GAN), a two-stage, hyperparameter-free gradient modification technique applied as a pre-processor before the optimizer update.
It computes a global gradient standard deviation, $s_t$, from an 'eligible set' of multi-dimensional gradient tensors (e.g., weights), excluding bias vectors and scalar parameters.

An autoscale multiplier, $a_t$, is derived from an adaptive transformation of $|\log s_t|$, designed to diminish over time in alignment with the observed global gradient dynamics, followed by layer-wise zero-centering and uniform scaling by $a_t$ across eligible layers.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at the University of Southern California developed Gradient Autoscaled Normalization, a hyperparameter-free method that scales gradients based on their observed globally decreasing standard deviation during training, addressing instability issues in layer-wise normalization. This approach improved Top-1 test accuracy on ResNet-20 by 2.02% and ResNet-56 by 1.28% on CIFAR-100 against an AdamW baseline, while maintaining performance on VGG-16-BN.

title: Insights from Gradient Dynamics: Gradient Autoscaled Normalization

abstract: Recent advances in spectral optimization, notably Muon, have demonstrated that constraining update steps to the Stiefel manifold can significantly accelerate training and improve generalization. However, Muon implicitly assumes an isotropic optimization landscape, enforcing a uniform spectral update norm across all eigen-directions. We argue that this "egalitarian" constraint is suboptimal for Deep Neural Networks, where the curvature spectrum is known to be highly heavy-tailed and ill-conditioned. In such landscapes, Muon risks amplifying instabilities in high-curvature directions while limiting necessary progress in flat directions. In this work, we propose \textbf{Mousse} (\textbf{M}uon \textbf{O}ptimization \textbf{U}tilizing \textbf{S}hampoo's \textbf{S}tructural \textbf{E}stimation), a novel optimizer that reconciles the structural stability of spectral methods with the geometric adaptivity of second-order preconditioning. Instead of applying Newton-Schulz orthogonalization directly to the momentum matrix, Mousse operates in a whitened coordinate system induced by Kronecker-factored statistics (derived from Shampoo). Mathematically, we formulate Mousse as the solution to a spectral steepest descent problem constrained by an anisotropic trust region, where the optimal update is derived via the polar decomposition of the whitened gradient. Empirical results across language models ranging from 160M to 800M parameters demonstrate that Mousse consistently outperforms Muon, achieving around $\sim$12\% reduction in training steps with negligible computational overhead.

keyInsights: Reconciling spectral stability with geometric adaptivity via curvature-aware preconditioning is crucial for overcoming Muon's isotropic limitations in anisotropic loss landscapes.

Engineering techniques like Trace Normalization for covariance matrices and a milder exponent for Spectral Tempering ($\alpha=0.125$) are essential for numerical stability and effective curvature correction.

Gradient grafting, which involves controlling the update norm through an auxiliary optimizer, prevents continuous downward drift of the update RMS norm, ensuring stable and efficient convergence in later training stages.
originalProblem: The Muon optimizer, while improving training acceleration and generalization, implicitly assumes an isotropic optimization landscape, applying a uniform spectral update norm across all eigen-directions.
Deep neural networks exhibit highly anisotropic and ill-conditioned curvature spectra, making Muon's isotropic constraint suboptimal for efficient exploration of the loss landscape.
This mismatch can amplify instabilities in high-curvature directions while limiting necessary progress in flatter directions, hindering overall training efficiency.
results: Mousse consistently achieved lower final validation loss (e.g., approximately 0.012 reduction for 800M parameter models) and required roughly 12% fewer training steps to reach comparable loss levels than the Muon baseline.

The optimizer maintained training speeds nearly identical to Muon, incurring a negligible wall-clock time overhead of approximately 3%, while outperforming other curvature-corrected methods like SOAP in efficiency.

Mousse demonstrated efficient memory usage, with a footprint about 1.05 times that of Muon, and experiments showed that single-sided preconditioners could achieve comparable performance with reduced computational and memory costs.
solution: Mousse integrates second-order preconditioning from Shampoo with Muon's spectral constraints, performing Newton-Schulz orthogonalization in a whitened coordinate system rather than on raw momentum.
The optimizer formulates updates as a dual-norm maximization problem under anisotropic geometry, using Kronecker-factored approximations of the Hessian to derive layer-wise whitening factors.
It applies these whitening factors ($L^{-1/4}$ and $R^{-1/4}$) to precondition the gradient before applying Muon's `msign` operation, followed by unwhitening to determine the parameter update.
paper_summary.summary: Mousse, an optimizer developed by researchers from Shanghai Jiao Tong University, Shanghai AI Laboratory, and Fudan University, enhances the Muon optimizer by integrating curvature-aware preconditioning. It addresses Muon's implicit assumption of an isotropic optimization landscape by adapting updates to the anisotropic geometry of deep neural networks, achieving approximately a 0.012 reduction in final validation loss and requiring 12% fewer training steps than Muon for comparable performance with minimal computational overhead.

title: Mousse: Rectifying the Geometry of Muon with Curvature-Aware Preconditioning

abstract: The $\mu$-parameterization ($\mu$P) provides a principled foundation for large language model (LLM) training by prescribing width-independent learning dynamics, which in turn enables predictable scaling behavior and robust hyperparameter transfer across model sizes. A central requirement of $\mu$P is the satisfaction of certain spectral conditions on weight matrices, which ensure consistent feature learning and optimization behavior as model width grows. While these conditions are well understood in theory, guaranteeing their validity in practical training for matrix-based optimizers such as Muon is still under studied. Existing works that study Muon under $\mu$P exhibit important limitations: they either do not ensure that the spectral conditions hold throughout the entire training horizon, or require repeated spectral normalization (or Newton-Schulz iterations) applied to both weights and updates, leading to significant computational overhead and reduced practicality. In this work, we show how to reliably guarantee the spectral conditions required by $\mu$P for Muon during the entire training process. Our key insight is that for moderately large models, maintaining spectral control at the level of optimizer updates alone is sufficient to preserve $\mu$P-compatible scaling, eliminating the need for explicit spectral normalization of the weights. Based on this principle, we develop a variant of Muon, namely Muon++, that satisfies spectral condition throughout the training process. Our results bridge the gap between the theoretical promises of $\mu$P and the practical deployment of matrix-based optimizers in long-horizon training. We also take the first step towards an adaptive spectral condition by incorporating data-dependent effects, making it better suited for long-horizon LLM training.
title: Towards a Principled Muon under $μ\mathsf{P}$: Ensuring Spectral Conditions throughout Training

abstract: Optimization lies at the core of modern deep learning, yet existing methods often face a fundamental trade-off between adapting to problem geometry and leveraging curvature utilization. Steepest descent algorithms adapt to different geometries through norm choices but remain strictly first-order, whereas quasi-Newton and adaptive optimizers incorporate curvature information but are restricted to Frobenius geometry, limiting their applicability across diverse architectures. In this work, we propose a unified framework generalizing steepest descent, quasi-Newton methods, and adaptive methods through the novel notion of preconditioned matrix norms. This abstraction reveals that widely used optimizers such as SGD and Adam, as well as more advanced approaches like Muon and KL-Shampoo, and recent hybrids including SOAP and SPlus, all emerge as special cases of the same principle. Within this framework, we provide the first systematic treatment of affine and scale invariance in the matrix-parameterized setting, establishing necessary and sufficient conditions under generalized norms. Building on this foundation, we introduce two new methods, $\texttt{MuAdam}$ and $\texttt{MuAdam-SANIA}$, which combine the spectral geometry of Muon with Adam-style preconditioning. Our experiments demonstrate that these optimizers are competitive with, and in some cases outperform, existing state-of-the-art methods. Our code is available at this https URL

organization_info.name: Mohamed bin Zayed University of Artificial Intelligence
Basic Research of Artificial Intelligence Laboratory

title: Preconditioned Norms: A Unified Framework for Steepest Descent, Quasi-Newton and Adaptive Methods

abstract: Maintaining numerical stability in machine learning models is crucial for their reliability and performance. One approach to maintain stability of a network layer is to integrate the condition number of the weight matrix as a regularizing term into the optimization algorithm. However, due to its discontinuous nature and lack of differentiability the condition number is not suitable for a gradient descent approach. This paper introduces a novel regularizer that is provably differentiable almost everywhere and promotes matrices with low condition numbers. In particular, we derive a formula for the gradient of this regularizer which can be easily implemented and integrated into existing optimization algorithms. We show the advantages of this approach for noisy classification and denoising of MNIST images.

organization_info.name: Acoustics Research Institute, Austrian Academy of Sciences

title: (Almost) Smooth Sailing: Towards Numerical Stability of Neural Networks Through Differentiable Regularization of the Condition Number

abstract: Physics-informed neural networks and neural operators often suffer from severe optimization difficulties caused by ill-conditioned gradients, multi-scale spectral behavior, and stiffness induced by physical constraints. Recently, the Muon optimizer has shown promise by performing orthogonalized updates in the singular-vector basis of the gradient, thereby improving geometric conditioning. However, its unit-singular-value updates may lead to overly aggressive steps and lack explicit stability guarantees when applied to physics-informed learning. In this work, we propose SpecMuon, a spectral-aware optimizer that integrates Muon's orthogonalized geometry with a mode-wise relaxed scalar auxiliary variable (RSAV) mechanism. By decomposing matrix-valued gradients into singular modes and applying RSAV updates individually along dominant spectral directions, SpecMuon adaptively regulates step sizes according to the global loss energy while preserving Muon's scale-balancing properties. This formulation interprets optimization as a multi-mode gradient flow and enables principled control of stiff spectral components. We establish rigorous theoretical properties of SpecMuon, including a modified energy dissipation law, positivity and boundedness of auxiliary variables, and global convergence with a linear rate under the Polyak-Lojasiewicz condition. Numerical experiments on physics-informed neural networks, DeepONets, and fractional PINN-DeepONets demonstrate that SpecMuon achieves faster convergence and improved stability compared with Adam, AdamW, and the original Muon optimizer on benchmark problems such as the one-dimensional Burgers equation and fractional partial differential equations.
title: Muon with Spectral Guidance: Efficient Optimization for Scientific Machine Learning

abstract: The natural gradient method is widely used in statistical optimization, but its standard formulation assumes a Euclidean parameter space. This paper proposes an inversion-free stochastic natural gradient method for probability distributions whose parameters lie on a Riemannian manifold. The manifold setting offers several advantages: one can implicitly enforce parameter constraints such as positive definiteness and orthogonality, ensure parameters are identifiable, or guarantee regularity properties of the objective like geodesic convexity. Building on an intrinsic formulation of the Fisher information matrix (FIM) on a manifold, our method maintains an online approximation of the inverse FIM, which is efficiently updated at quadratic cost using score vectors sampled at successive iterates. In the Riemannian setting, these score vectors belong to different tangent spaces and must be combined using transport operations. We prove almost-sure convergence rates of $O(\log{s}/s^\alpha)$ for the squared distance to the minimizer when the step size exponent $\alpha >2/3$. We also establish almost-sure rates for the approximate FIM, which now accumulates transport-based errors. A limited-memory variant of the algorithm with sub-quadratic storage complexity is proposed. Finally, we demonstrate the effectiveness of our method relative to its Euclidean counterparts on variational Bayes with Gaussian approximations and normalizing flows.

keyInsights: An intrinsic, inversion-free approach effectively generalizes natural gradient descent to Riemannian manifolds by combining online FIM approximation with vector transport for tangent space alignment.

Leveraging the intrinsic geometry of parameter spaces (e.g., Bures-Wasserstein, Stiefel manifolds) significantly enhances optimization stability and performance for models with constrained parameters.
Almost-sure convergence rates can be rigorously established for stochastic natural gradient on manifolds, accounting for errors from retractions and vector transport operations.
originalProblem: Standard optimization methods for parametric probability distributions often disregard the intrinsic geometric structure of the parameter space or struggle with parameters on constrained manifolds (e.g., SPD, Stiefel).
Computing the natural gradient typically requires analytically intractable or computationally expensive O(d^3) inversion of the Fisher Information Matrix (FIM) at each step.
Some models exhibit non-identifiability in Euclidean parameterizations, leading to ill-conditioned or singular FIMs.
results: The inversion-free natural gradient method achieved comparable performance to exact natural gradient while outperforming standard gradient descent in variational Bayes with Gaussian approximations.

Leveraging the Bures-Wasserstein manifold for covariance matrices led to faster convergence and better ELBO values, demonstrating increased stability compared to Euclidean parameterizations, especially in higher dimensions.

On normalizing flows with Stiefel manifold constraints, the method yielded the lowest negative ELBO across datasets, surpassing Riemannian Stochastic Gradient Descent and an extrinsic natural gradient approach.
solution: The authors developed an intrinsic, inversion-free stochastic natural gradient method operating directly on general Riemannian manifolds.
An online approximation of the inverse Fisher Information Matrix (FIM) is maintained and updated using rank-one updates (Sherman-Morrison formula) with O(d^2) complexity, avoiding direct inversion.

Vector transport operations are integrated to correctly move the inverse FIM approximation between varying tangent spaces, and a limited-memory variant reduces complexity to O(dK) for high-dimensional problems.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers developed an inversion-free stochastic natural gradient method for optimizing objective functions on general Riemannian manifolds, providing almost-sure convergence rates for parameter iterates and the approximate Fisher Information Matrix. The method demonstrated improved stability and performance in variational Bayes with Gaussian approximations and normalizing flows, especially when leveraging intrinsic manifold geometries like the Bures-Wasserstein and Stiefel manifolds.

title: Inversion-Free Natural Gradient Descent on Riemannian Manifolds

abstract: The field of deep generative modeling has grown rapidly in the last few years. With the availability of massive amounts of training data coupled with advances in scalable unsupervised learning paradigms, recent large-scale generative models show tremendous promise in synthesizing high-resolution images and text, as well as structured data such as videos and molecules. However, we argue that current large-scale generative AI models exhibit several fundamental shortcomings that hinder their widespread adoption across domains. In this work, our objective is to identify these issues and highlight key unresolved challenges in modern generative AI paradigms that should be addressed to further enhance their capabilities, versatility, and reliability. By identifying these challenges, we aim to provide researchers with insights for exploring fruitful research directions, thus fostering the development of more robust and accessible generative AI solutions.
title: On the Challenges and Opportunities in Generative AI

abstract: The Frobenius norm is a frequent choice of norm for matrices. In particular, the underlying Frobenius inner product is typically used to evaluate the gradient of an objective with respect to matrix variable, such as those occuring in the training of neural networks. We provide a broader view on the Frobenius norm and inner product for linear maps or matrices, and establish their dependence on inner products in the domain and co-domain spaces. This shows that the classical Frobenius norm is merely one special element of a family of more general Frobenius-type norms. The significant extra freedom furnished by this realization can be used, among other things, to precondition neural network training.
title: Frobenius-Type Norms and Inner Products of Matrices and Linear Maps with Applications to Neural Network Training

abstract: The growing adoption of spectrum-aware matrix-valued optimizers such as Muon and Shampoo in deep learning motivates a systematic study of their generalization properties and, in particular, when they might outperform competitive algorithms. We approach this question by introducing appropriate simplifying abstractions as follows: First, we use imbalanced data as a testbed. Second, we study the canonical form of such optimizers, which is Spectral Gradient Descent (SpecGD) -- each update step is $UV^T$ where $U\Sigma V^T$ is the truncated SVD of the gradient. Third, within this framework we identify a canonical setting for which we precisely quantify when SpecGD outperforms vanilla Euclidean GD. For a Gaussian mixture data model and both linear and bilinear models, we show that unlike GD, which prioritizes learning dominant principal components of the data first, SpecGD learns all principal components of the data at equal rates. We demonstrate how this translates to a growing gap in class balanced loss favoring SpecGD early in training and further show that the gap remains consistent even when the GD counterpart uses adaptive step-sizes via normalization. By extending the analysis to deep linear models, we show that depth amplifies these effects. We empirically verify our theoretical findings on a variety of imbalanced datasets. Our experiments compare practical variants of spectral methods, like Muon and Shampoo, against their Euclidean counterparts and Adam. The results validate our findings that these spectral optimizers achieve superior generalization by promoting a more balanced learning of the data's underlying components.
title: How Muon's Spectral Design Benefits Generalization: A Study on Imbalanced Data

abstract:
Optimal Transport has sparked vivid interest in recent years, in particular
thanks to the Wasserstein distance, which provides a geometrically sensible and
intuitive way of comparing probability measures. For computational reasons, the
Sliced Wasserstein (SW) distance was introduced as an alternative to the
Wasserstein distance, and has seen uses for training generative Neural Networks
(NNs). While convergence of Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) has been observed
practically in such a setting, there is to our knowledge no theoretical
guarantee for this observation. Leveraging recent works on convergence of SGD
on non-smooth and non-convex functions by Bianchi et al. (2022), we aim to
bridge that knowledge gap, and provide a realistic context under which
fixed-step SGD trajectories for the SW loss on NN parameters converge. More
precisely, we show that the trajectories approach the set of (sub)-gradient
flow equations as the step decreases. Under stricter assumptions, we show a
much stronger convergence result for noised and projected SGD schemes, namely
that the long-run limits of the trajectories approach a set of generalised
critical points of the loss function.
title: Convergence of SGD for Training Neural Networks with Sliced Wasserstein  Losses

abstract:
We introduce Manifold Sobolev Informed Neural Optimization (MSINO), a curvature aware training framework for neural networks defined on Riemannian manifolds. The method replaces standard Euclidean derivative supervision with a covariant Sobolev loss that aligns gradients using parallel transport and improves stability via a Laplace Beltrami smoothness regularization term.
Building on classical results in Riemannian optimization and Sobolev theory on manifolds, we derive geometry dependent constants that yield (i) a Descent Lemma with a manifold Sobolev smoothness constant, (ii) a Sobolev Polyak Lojasiewicz inequality giving linear convergence guarantees for Riemannian gradient descent and stochastic gradient descent under explicit step size bounds, and (iii) a two step Newton Sobolev method with local quadratic contraction in curvature controlled neighborhoods.
Unlike prior Sobolev training in Euclidean space, MSINO provides training time guarantees that explicitly track curvature and transported Jacobians. Applications include surface imaging, physics informed learning settings, and robotics on Lie groups such as SO(3) and SE(3). The framework unifies value and gradient based learning with curvature aware convergence guarantees for neural training on manifolds.
title: MSINO: Curvature-Aware Sobolev Optimization for Manifold Neural Networks

abstract: Wasserstein gradient flow has emerged as a promising approach to solve optimization problems over the space of probability distributions. A recent trend is to use the well-known JKO scheme in combination with input convex neural networks to numerically implement the proximal step. The most challenging step, in this setup, is to evaluate functions involving density explicitly, such as entropy, in terms of samples. This paper builds on the recent works with a slight but crucial difference: we propose to utilize a variational formulation of the objective function formulated as maximization over a parametric class of functions. Theoretically, the proposed variational formulation allows the construction of gradient flows directly for empirical distributions with a well-defined and meaningful objective function. Computationally, this approach replaces the computationally expensive step in existing methods, to handle objective functions involving density, with inner loop updates that only require a small batch of samples and scale well with the dimension. The performance and scalability of the proposed method are illustrated with the aid of several numerical experiments involving high-dimensional synthetic and real datasets.
title: Variational Wasserstein gradient flow

abstract: Many tasks in machine learning and signal processing can be solved by minimizing a convex function of a measure. This includes sparse spikes deconvolution or training a neural network with a single hidden layer. For these problems, we study a simple minimization method: the unknown measure is discretized into a mixture of particles and a continuous-time gradient descent is performed on their weights and positions. This is an idealization of the usual way to train neural networks with a large hidden layer. We show that, when initialized correctly and in the many-particle limit, this gradient flow, although non-convex, converges to global minimizers. The proof involves Wasserstein gradient flows, a by-product of optimal transport theory. Numerical experiments show that this asymptotic behavior is already at play for a reasonable number of particles, even in high dimension.

keyInsights: Non-convex finite-particle gradient descent can be understood as a discretization of an underlying convex problem defined over the space of measures.
Positive homogeneity of problem components (e.g., feature maps, regularizers) is crucial for the continuous-time gradient flow to avoid non-optimal stationary points.
A "separation" property in the initial measure's support is preserved throughout the Wasserstein gradient flow evolution, enabling the flow to converge to global minima.
originalProblem: Explaining why non-convex particle gradient descent methods, commonly used for over-parameterized models like neural networks, consistently find global minima despite their non-convex nature.
Bridging the theoretical gap between the infinite-dimensional, often convex, formulation of these problems in the space of measures and their finite-dimensional, non-convex discretizations.
Providing a generic theoretical framework for global convergence, distinct from approaches focusing on specific neural network architectures or the effects of stochasticity.
results: A many-particle limit theorem rigorously demonstrates that continuous-time particle gradient flows converge to unique Wasserstein gradient flows as the number of particles tends to infinity.

Wasserstein gradient flows are proven to converge to global minimizers for problems exhibiting 2-homogeneity (e.g., ReLU neural networks) or partial 1-homogeneity (e.g., sparse deconvolution, sigmoid neural networks), provided specific initialization conditions are met.

Numerical experiments on synthetic data illustrate that particle gradient flows achieve significantly lower excess loss for a given particle count compared to convex optimization on a fixed grid, emphasizing the importance of particle movement.
solution: The authors generalize a class of machine learning problems as convex minimization problems over signed measures, then transform them into equivalent problems over non-negative probability measures on an augmented parameter space.

They define continuous-time Particle Gradient Flows (PGF) for finite particles and introduce Wasserstein Gradient Flows (WGF) as their infinite-particle (continuous-measure) analog, operating in the space of probability measures with the W2 Wasserstein distance.

Global convergence is established by analyzing the properties of WGFs under conditions of positive homogeneity of the objective function and specific "separation" properties in the initial measure's support.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from INRIA, ENS, and PSL Research University present a theoretical framework using optimal transport and Wasserstein gradient flows to explain why gradient descent converges to global minima in over-parameterized non-convex models. Their work demonstrates that under conditions of homogeneity and specific initializations, the many-particle limit of gradient descent behaves as a globally convergent flow on the space of measures.

title: On the Global Convergence of Gradient Descent for Over-parameterized Models using Optimal Transport

abstract: Muon is a recently proposed optimizer that enforces orthogonality in parameter updates by projecting gradients onto the Stiefel manifold, leading to stable and efficient training in large-scale deep neural networks. Meanwhile, the previously reported results indicated that stochastic noise in practical machine learning may exhibit heavy-tailed behavior, violating the bounded-variance assumption. In this paper, we consider the problem of minimizing a nonconvex Hölder-smooth empirical risk that works well with the heavy-tailed stochastic noise. We then show that Muon converges to a stationary point of the empirical risk under the boundedness condition accounting for heavy-tailed stochastic noise. In addition, we show that Muon converges faster than mini-batch SGD.
title: Muon Converges under Heavy-Tailed Noise: Nonconvex Hölder-Smooth Empirical Risk Minimization

abstract:
The metric $d(A,B)=\left[ \tr\, A+\tr\,
B-2\tr(A^{1/2}BA^{1/2})^{1/2}\right]^{1/2}$ on the manifold of $n\times n$
positive definite matrices arises in various optimisation problems, in quantum
information and in the theory of optimal transport. It is also related to
Riemannian geometry. In the first part of this paper we study this metric from
the perspective of matrix analysis, simplifying and unifying various proofs.
Then we develop a theory of a mean of two, and a barycentre of several,
positive definite matrices with respect to this metric. We explain some recent
work on a fixed point iteration for computing this Wasserstein barycentre. Our
emphasis is on ideas natural to matrix analysis.
title: On the Bures-Wasserstein distance between positive definite matrices

abstract: We study the estimation of optimal transport (OT) maps between an arbitrary source probability measure and a log-concave target probability measure. Our contributions are twofold. First, we propose a new evolution equation in the set of transport maps. It can be seen as the gradient flow of a lift of some user-chosen divergence (e.g., the KL divergence, or relative entropy) to the space of transport maps, constrained to the convex set of optimal transport maps. We prove the existence of long-time solutions to this flow as well as its convergence toward the OT map as time goes to infinity, under standard convexity conditions on the divergence. Second, we study the practical implementation of this constrained gradient flow. We propose two time-discrete computational schemes-one explicit, one implicit-, and we prove the convergence of the latter to the OT map as time goes to infinity. We then parameterize the OT maps with convexity-constrained neural networks and train them with these discretizations of the constrained gradient flow. We show that this is equivalent to performing a natural gradient descent of the lift of the chosen divergence in the neural networks' parameter space. Empirically, our scheme outperforms the standard Euclidean gradient descent methods used to train convexity-constrained neural networks in terms of approximation results for the OT map and convergence stability, and it still yields better results than the same approach combined with the widely used adam optimizer.

keyInsights: The constrained gradient flow ensures the evolving map remains a gradient of a convex function, providing strong theoretical guarantees for existence and convergence, including exponential rates independent of dimension for strongly convex divergences.

The parameterized version of the constrained gradient flow is equivalent to an L²_ϱ₀-natural gradient flow, which inherently accounts for the underlying Riemannian geometry and offers improved optimization properties.

Leveraging the intrinsic geometric structure of OT maps through this constrained flow leads to more stable and accurate optimization, mitigating convergence to suboptimal local minima encountered by standard methods.
originalProblem: Estimating optimal transport (OT) maps between probability measures becomes computationally intractable in high-dimensional settings due to the "curse of dimensionality".
Current high-dimensional methods often relax critical OT constraints or optimality conditions, leading to less precise solutions.
Parameterizing OT maps with neural networks, particularly convexity-constrained ones, still suffers from convergence to suboptimal local minima when using standard Euclidean gradient descent.
results: The proposed constrained gradient flow schemes (explicit and implicit) consistently achieved lower Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD) values (around 0.02) and higher accuracy compared to standard Euclidean gradient descent.

These methods demonstrated superior robustness, outperforming even Adam-optimized Euclidean gradient descent with mean MMDs of 0.019-0.024 versus Adam's 0.029, and showing fewer convergences to suboptimal solutions.

The explicit constrained gradient flow scheme achieved the best overall performance, with the lowest mean MMD (0.0194) and standard deviation, highlighting its efficiency and consistency in approximating OT maps.
solution: Introduce a novel constrained gradient flow in the space of transport maps, defined as the L²_ϱ₀-gradient flow of a lifted divergence functional, projected onto the set of gradients of convex functions.
Develop explicit and implicit time-discretization schemes for this continuous flow, allowing for practical implementation.
Parameterize the optimal transport maps as gradients of convexity-constrained neural networks (e.g., Input Convex Neural Networks) to handle high-dimensional functional approximation.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at Université Gustave Eiffel propose a constrained gradient flow framework for learning optimal transport (OT) maps, which are parameterized by gradients of convex neural networks. This approach offers theoretical convergence guarantees, including exponential rates independent of dimension, and empirically yields more accurate and stable OT map estimations compared to standard gradient descent methods.

title: Learning Monge maps by lifting and constraining Wasserstein gradient flows

abstract:
Normalization Layers (NLs) are widely used in modern deep-learning
architectures. Despite their apparent simplicity, their effect on optimization
is not yet fully understood. This paper introduces a spherical framework to
study the optimization of neural networks with NLs from a geometric
perspective. Concretely, the radial invariance of groups of parameters, such as
filters for convolutional neural networks, allows to translate the optimization
steps on the $L_2$ unit hypersphere. This formulation and the associated
geometric interpretation shed new light on the training dynamics. Firstly, the
first effective learning rate expression of Adam is derived. Then the
demonstration that, in the presence of NLs, performing Stochastic Gradient
Descent (SGD) alone is actually equivalent to a variant of Adam constrained to
the unit hypersphere, stems from the framework. Finally, this analysis outlines
phenomena that previous variants of Adam act on and their importance in the
optimization process are experimentally validated.
title: Spherical Perspective on Learning with Normalization Layers

abstract: Spectral gradient methods, such as the Muon optimizer, modify gradient updates by preserving directional information while discarding scale, and have shown strong empirical performance in deep learning. We investigate the mechanisms underlying these gains through a dynamical analysis of a nonlinear phase retrieval model with anisotropic Gaussian inputs, equivalent to training a two-layer neural network with the quadratic activation and fixed second-layer weights. Focusing on a spiked covariance setting where the dominant variance direction is orthogonal to the signal, we show that gradient descent (GD) suffers from a variance-induced misalignment: during the early escaping stage, the high-variance but uninformative spike direction is multiplicatively amplified, degrading alignment with the true signal under strong anisotropy. In contrast, spectral gradient descent (SpecGD) removes this spike amplification effect, leading to stable alignment and accelerated noise contraction. Numerical experiments confirm the theory and show that these phenomena persist under broader anisotropic covariances.

keyInsights: SpecGD performs a sign-based gradient update in an adaptive basis, effectively decoupling the magnitude of gradient components from their direction, which provides robustness against anisotropic data.

Gradient Descent (GD) is prone to 'variance-induced misalignment,' where high-variance, uninformative directions are disproportionately amplified during early learning stages, delaying true signal alignment.

SpecGD mitigates this amplification, exhibiting a more 'isotropic expansion' during initial learning and enabling faster, stable alignment with the true signal even in strongly anisotropic environments.
originalProblem: The theoretical mechanisms behind the empirical success of spectral gradient methods like Muon, beyond simple gradient rescaling, remained largely unexplored.
Traditional first-order optimizers often struggle with anisotropic data distributions, where high-variance but uninformative directions can impede effective learning.
Prior theoretical analyses of spectral gradient methods were primarily limited to linear models, not fully capturing their behavior in nonlinear feature learning contexts.
results: SpecGD's initial growth stage (Stage I) has a transition time of O(1), independent of data dimension or spike strength, with coefficients growing quadratically, indicating efficient noise contraction.
Gradient Descent's corresponding Stage I transition time is significantly slower, scaling as O((learning_rate * spike_intensity)^-1 * log(dimension)), showing its sensitivity to anisotropy.

SpecGD achieves full alignment with the true signal (alignment approaching 1) after O(1/learning_rate) iterations, demonstrating its ability to robustly navigate anisotropic loss landscapes where GD often misaligns or requires impractically small learning rates.
solution: Researchers developed a theoretical framework analyzing Spectral Gradient Descent (SpecGD) in a nonlinear phase retrieval model, which functions as a proxy for shallow neural networks.
They specifically constructed a challenging anisotropic input setting where a dominant, high-variance data 'spike' is orthogonal to the true signal.

The dynamics were rigorously reduced to a three-dimensional invariant manifold, enabling the derivation and comparison of ordinary differential equations (ODEs) for both SpecGD and Gradient Descent (GD).
paper_summary.summary: This study explains how Spectral Gradient Descent (SpecGD) navigates anisotropic data by demonstrating its sign-based, adaptive-basis updates prevent uninformative high-variance directions from dominating the learning process. SpecGD achieves stable and rapid alignment with the true signal in a nonlinear phase retrieval model, outperforming Gradient Descent which suffers from anisotropy-driven misalignment.

title: Spectral Gradient Descent Mitigates Anisotropy-Driven Misalignment: A Case Study in Phase Retrieval

abstract:
Overparameterized models trained with (stochastic) gradient descent are
ubiquitous in modern machine learning. These large models achieve unprecedented
performance on test data, but their theoretical understanding is still limited.
In this paper, we take a step towards filling this gap by adopting an
optimization perspective. More precisely, we study the implicit regularization
properties of the gradient flow "algorithm" for estimating the parameters of a
deep diagonal neural network. Our main contribution is showing that this
gradient flow induces a mirror flow dynamic on the model, meaning that it is
biased towards a specific solution of the problem depending on the
initialization of the network. Along the way, we prove several properties of
the trajectory.
title: Optimization Insights into Deep Diagonal Linear Networks

abstract: Optimal transport and its related problems, including optimal partial transport, have proven to be valuable tools in machine learning for computing meaningful distances between probability or positive measures. This success has led to a growing interest in defining transport-based distances that allow for comparing signed measures and, more generally, multi-channeled signals. Transport $\mathrm{L}^{p}$ distances are notable extensions of the optimal transport framework to signed and possibly multi-channeled signals. In this paper, we introduce partial transport $\mathrm{L}^{p}$ distances as a new family of metrics for comparing generic signals, benefiting from the robustness of partial transport distances. We provide theoretical background such as the existence of optimal plans and the behavior of the distance in various limits. Furthermore, we introduce the sliced variation of these distances, which allows for rapid comparison of generic signals. Finally, we demonstrate the application of the proposed distances in signal class separability and nearest neighbor classification.

keyInsights: PTL_p provides enhanced robustness for comparing generic signals with partial overlaps, noise, or varying lengths, as it can selectively match relevant parts while disregarding non-corresponding segments.

The PTL_p metric is theoretically grounded, defining a proper metric space, and its behavior can be controlled by a parameter 'β' to interpolate between emphasizing domain alignment (L_p on common mass) and feature-space distribution similarity (OPT on feature pushforward measures).

The slicing approach for SPTL_p significantly reduces the computational burden from cubic to near-linear complexity in the number of samples, making the robust PTL_p framework practically viable for large-scale applications.
originalProblem: Existing Optimal Transport (OT) methods are limited to non-negative measures with equal total mass, severely restricting their application to generic multi-channel or signed signals.

Previous Transport L_p (TL_p) distances extended OT to generic signals but still required complete correspondences, leading to inaccuracies when comparing signals with partial overlaps or varying lengths.

Real-world signals often feature noise, missing segments, or only partial overlaps, necessitating a robust dissimilarity metric that naturally accommodates these characteristics without forcing full alignment.
The high computational complexity of full OT-based methods limits their practical applicability to large datasets or high-dimensional signals.
results: PTL_p demonstrated superior robustness in signal class separability on synthetic data with random noise, maintaining clearer decision boundaries compared to TL_p and L_p distances.
SPTL_p achieved competitive performance in 1-Nearest Neighbor classification, placing among the top methods on modified UCR datasets designed for prefix, subsequence, and suffix scenarios.
The sliced implementation (SPTL_p) drastically reduced the worst-case computational complexity from O(N^3(d+k)) for full PTL_p/TL_p to O(LN((d+k) + N + log(N))), enabling efficient computation.
solution: Introduced Partial Transport L_p (PTL_p) distances by extending the TL_p framework with Optimal Partial Transport (OPT) principles, allowing for partial correspondences and penalizing unmatched mass.
Formulated PTL_p by lifting generic multi-channel signals onto their graphs in a higher-dimensional space and defining the distance as an OPT problem between these lifted measures.

Developed Sliced Partial Transport L_p (SPTL_p) to reduce computational complexity by projecting high-dimensional lifted measures onto numerous one-dimensional lines and averaging the results of efficient 1D OPT problems.
paper_summary.summary: The paper introduces Partial Transport L_p (PTL_p) distances and their sliced variant (SPTL_p) for comparing generic multi-channel signals, handling partial correspondences and varying lengths more effectively than previous methods. This new metric, developed by researchers primarily at Vanderbilt University, was empirically shown to enhance signal class separability in noisy conditions and achieved competitive 1-Nearest Neighbor classification accuracy on modified UCR datasets.

title: PT$\mathrm{L}^{p}$: Partial Transport $\mathrm{L}^{p}$ Distances

abstract:
We consider learning two layer neural networks using stochastic gradient descent. The mean-field description of this learning dynamics approximates the evolution of the network weights by an evolution in the space of probability distributions in $R^D$ (where $D$ is the number of parameters associated to each neuron). This evolution can be defined through a partial differential equation or, equivalently, as the gradient flow in the Wasserstein space of probability distributions. Earlier work shows that (under some regularity assumptions), the mean field description is accurate as soon as the number of hidden units is much larger than the dimension $D$. In this paper we establish stronger and more general approximation guarantees.
